summary: Verify too_weak tasks by actually running the grading test against a deliberately-wrong/trivial answer

details.evidence: A trivially wrong answer that omits all toxics configuration (latency, bandwidth, timeout, slow_close) but has the correct proxy names, ports, and enabled=true passed all 8 grading tests. The task explicitly requires applying network toxics to each proxy, but none of the 8 tests check for toxics at all. Tests only verify: file exists, valid JSON, three service keys present, correct listen/upstream ports per service, all proxies enabled, and localhost addresses. Written config at /tmp/toxiproxy_config.json with just listen/upstream/enabled fields (no toxics) — all 8/8 tests passed in 0.02s.

A deliberately wrong answer {"total_slots": 1, "host_count": 1, "queue_count": 0} passed all 8 tests. The test file /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/univa_grid_engine_slots_hard/tests/test_outputs.py only checks: file exists, valid JSON, required fields present, no extra fields, all fields are integers, all values non-negative, total_slots > 0 and host_count > 0, and total_slots >= host_count. It never compares against the actual configuration files (exec_hosts.conf, queue.conf, etc.) which contain many hosts (20+ entries) with hundreds of total slots and multiple queues. Any answer with total_slots >= host_count >= 1 and queue_count >= 0 will pass all 8 tests regardless of correctness. Verified by running inside a Docker container built from the same Dockerfile pattern: 8/8 tests passed with the trivial wrong answer.

All 8 tests passed with a deliberately wrong verify_timing.py that claims valid=true and violations=0 without performing any actual timing constraint analysis. The trivial script only reads the log to count lines (for total_events) and writes {"valid": true, "violations": 0, "total_events": 328}. The test suite in /tests/test_outputs.py only checks structural validity (file exists, valid JSON, required fields, correct types, no extra fields, logical consistency between valid/violations, and total_events matches log line count). It never checks whether the violation count is actually correct or whether timing constraints were genuinely verified. A completely wrong answer — claiming no violations even if violations exist — passes all 8 tests.

Ran the test with two deliberately wrong answers: all-SAT and all-UNSAT. In both cases, 5 of 6 tests passed (format/structure checks) but `test_sat_results_correctness` correctly failed. The test uses pycosat as a fallback SAT solver to verify each claimed SAT/UNSAT status against the actual satisfiability of each CNF formula. For all-SAT, it caught `property_2.cnf: claimed SAT but actual status is UNSAT`. For all-UNSAT, it caught `property_1.cnf: claimed UNSAT but actual status is SAT`. The correctness test at `/home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/vampire_fof_proof_generation_medium/tests/test_outputs.py` (line 101-118) is robust: it calls `verify_cnf_satisfiability()` which uses pycosat to solve each formula and rejects mismatches. Total: 6 tests, wrong answer passed 5 (structural tests), failed 1 (correctness test).

Built and ran the Docker test with a deliberately wrong answer: {"occurs_check_violations": 0, "type_mismatches": 0, "valid_proofs": 0, "total_processed": 20}. All 9 tests passed. The grading in test_outputs.py (/home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/vampire_higher_order_reasoning_hard/tests/test_outputs.py) only validates: file exists, valid JSON, required fields present, integer types, total_processed==20, non-negative values, valid_proofs<=total_processed, violations<=total_processed, and exact field set. No test checks the actual correctness of occurs_check_violations, type_mismatches, or valid_proofs against ground-truth values derived from the log files. Any integer combination where total_processed==20 and all values are between 0-20 passes.

All 10 tests pass with a deliberately wrong answer: fixed_processor.py containing only `print("hello")` and results.json with fabricated hourly counts of 60/60/60 (total=180, wrong values that don't reflect any watermark logic). The tests only check: (1) file existence, (2) valid JSON, (3) correct structure, (4) ISO 8601 format on hour boundaries, (5) positive integers, (6) total_counted <= total_events AND total_counted > 0, (7) total_counted >= total_events * 0.5. No test verifies the actual per-window counts against expected values computed from the input data. Any fabricated output with valid-format hourly keys and counts summing to ≥92 (50% of 184 events) passes all tests. Run in Docker image built from the provided Dockerfile.

All 7 grading tests passed with a completely fabricated answer: exploit_class="com.fake.FakeClass" and target_command="echo hello". The test suite (/home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/weblogic_deserialization_exploit_medium/tests/test_outputs.py) only validates structural properties (file exists, valid JSON, required fields present, non-empty strings, dot in class name, exactly 2 fields) — it never checks that the extracted values actually match the real attack data in /workspace/evidence/t3_decoded.log. Any dotted Java class name and any non-empty command string passes all tests.
